title: PtP Wireless Link Guide
summary: Understand distance, frequency, antenna gain, extra losses, noise floor, and SNR margin for point-to-point wireless links.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a point-to-point wireless link has enough received signal and SNR margin for a reliable path.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document wireless design assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch RF, capacity, or margin pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning building-to-building wireless, directional bridge links, temporary wireless backhaul, or long-distance RF paths.
bullets: Early wireless design planning.
Expansion review or refresh planning.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting support.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical wireless planning terms.

items.label: Distance (ft)
items.description: The physical path distance between the two link endpoints.
items.whyItMatters: Distance drives path loss and affects the required antenna/power margin.
items.commonMistake: Using rough walking distance instead of the actual RF path.

items.label: Frequency (GHz)
items.description: The operating frequency of the point-to-point link.
items.whyItMatters: Frequency affects path loss, interference exposure, antenna choice, and availability.
items.commonMistake: Changing bands without recalculating path loss and margin.

items.label: TX Power (dBm)
items.description: The transmitter output power at the sending endpoint.
items.whyItMatters: Transmit power contributes to received signal but must be evaluated with antenna gain and legal EIRP.
items.commonMistake: Trying to fix a bad path only by increasing transmit power.

items.label: TX Antenna Gain (dBi)
items.description: The gain of the transmitting antenna toward the far endpoint.
items.whyItMatters: Directional gain improves link margin when alignment is correct.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting that high-gain antennas require accurate alignment.

items.label: RX Antenna Gain (dBi)
items.description: The gain of the receiving antenna toward the transmitter.
items.whyItMatters: Receive gain improves the signal collected at the far end of the link.
items.commonMistake: Assuming gain helps if mounting or aiming is poor.

items.label: Other Losses (dB)
items.description: Additional loss from cables, connectors, obstruction, foliage, rain fade, misalignment, or installation conditions.
items.whyItMatters: Losses reduce the final received signal and can erase fade margin.
items.commonMistake: Leaving losses at zero for real outdoor installations.

items.label: Noise Floor (dBm)
items.description: The background RF noise level at the receiver.
items.whyItMatters: Noise floor determines how much SNR remains after received signal is calculated.
items.commonMistake: Checking signal level but not the channel noise.

items.label: Target SNR (dB)
items.description: The SNR needed for the intended reliability or modulation rate.
items.whyItMatters: Target SNR sets the margin requirement for the final link result.
items.commonMistake: Expecting high throughput from a link with barely enough SNR.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the point-to-point link appears to have useful margin. Watch means path, antennas, or noise assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the link may need better alignment, cleaner spectrum, stronger antennas, shorter path, or a different band.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but RF conditions, client behavior, or operating margin should be reviewed.
Risk: add capacity, reduce assumptions, improve placement, or validate with a survey before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Wireless planning problems usually come from treating Wi-Fi like a fixed cable instead of a shared RF environment affected by airtime, interference, device capability, channel reuse, and roaming behavior.
bullets: Sizing only for coverage and ignoring capacity.
Using ideal vendor rates as if they are sustained usable throughput.
Ignoring noise floor, channel overlap, client mix, and airtime contention.
Failing to document assumptions before AP count, channel plan, or antenna placement is finalized.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace a wireless survey, manufacturer documentation, controller validation, spectrum analysis, or project-specific RF engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications and controller guidance still matter.
Client device capability can be the limiting factor even when AP hardware is strong.
Walls, mounting height, interference, antenna pattern, and channel reuse can change the final result.
A wireless design with margin is usually easier to support, tune, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal wireless design review, site survey validation, manufacturer guidance, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual AP model, antenna pattern, client mix, controller settings, and site survey results.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for coverage, speed, roaming, and reliability.
Document assumptions before implementation or final AP placement.
